D3 Chemistry L4 Analyze Reference

Amethyst — Fe-Substituted Quartz Under Radiation

amethyst is quartz with Fe substituting for Si; natural radiation converts Fe³⁺ → Fe⁴⁺ + hole, the [FeO₄]⁰ centre absorbs yellow-green light and the crystal looks deep purple

Amethyst is a quartz variety with trace iron substituting Si in the SiO₄ tetrahedra. As-grown the Fe³⁺ is in [FeO₄]⁻; natural ionising radiation removes an electron giving the paramagnetic [FeO₄]⁰ hole centre (formally Fe⁴⁺). Its broad d-orbital absorption peaks around 550 nm — yellow-green — so the transmitted complement is the iconic purple. Heating to ~400 °C reverses the colour, turning amethyst into citrine (Fe³⁺ + heat → orange Fe³⁺ trapped).

Drag dose and anneal temperature. The crystal shifts from clear → pale → deep purple; high anneal turns it citrine-orange.
